text: The surgeon carefully dissects the omentum from the gallbladder, ensuring the transverse colon is not injured during the process. The duodenum, which is adherent to the gallbladder, is also carefully separated.

The surgeon removes fibrofatty tissue from the hepatocystic triangle, utilizing firefly mode to visually identify the common bile duct. The intensity of the imaging is adjusted to enhance the background contrast.

The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety and places a clip below the infundibulum on the gallbladder after milking the cystic duct. The cystic duct is incised, and a cholangiogram catheter is inserted through an introducer. A wire is placed through the five French cholangiocatheter for stiffness, facilitating passage through the cystic duct. Arm one of the robotic system is undocked to allow the C-arm to approach from the patient's right side, while a grasper in arm four holds the cholangiocatheter in place. Full contrast is injected to perform the cholangiogram.

The surgeon observes filling defects in the common bile duct while contrast flows into the duodenum. A larger catheter is used, and a balloon is inserted to dilate the ducts. Initially, the balloon faces difficulty passing due to an obstructing stone. The balloon is inflated and held for three minutes, achieving eight millimeters of dilation, allowing the choledochoscope to pass. The choledochoscope reveals the right and left hepatic branches and their bifurcation, with visible blood from the insertion.
The choledochoscope reveals a large stone located in the distal portion of the common bile duct.

The surgeon performs a choledochotomy for direct exploration of the common bile duct due to a large stone. A four-French Fogarty balloon is passed multiple times to dislodge and retrieve the stones. Fat is cleared from the anterior aspect of the common bile duct, with retraction facilitated by lifting the falciform ligament. The cystic duct is clipped, and the gallbladder is removed to improve visualization.

The surgeon performs a choledochotomy, followed by irrigation of the common bile duct, resulting in the immediate retrieval of several stones. The irrigation aids in visualization and helps the stones float for easier removal.

The surgeon performs a chole-docotomy anteriorly between the three and nine o'clock positions to avoid injuring the blood supply to the ducts. Continued irrigation aids in the retrieval of several stones, with a total of seven stones being removed from the common bile duct.

The surgeon uses the choledochoscope to inspect the common bile duct, ensuring all stones and debris have been removed. An irrigating catheter is then passed both proximally and distally to clear any remaining small debris.
One milligram of glucagon is administered to dilate the sphincter of Oddi, facilitating the closure of the choledochotomy after ensuring the common bile duct is clear of stones.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 monofilament absorbable suture to close the common bile duct in an interrupted fashion, ensuring the sutures do not cause narrowing due to the duct's dilation to 1.5 centimeters. The sutures appear smaller than they are due to ten times magnification.

After closing the choledochotomy, the surgeon removes clips from the cystic duct to perform a completion cholangiogram. The cholangiogram reveals resolution of filling defects, with contrast visibly flowing to the bifurcation of the right and left hepatic ducts and down the common bile duct into the duodenum. Clips are reapplied to the cystic duct, marking the completion of the procedure.
